ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn URI for French macaron; mapped to closest parent class 'cookie (food product)' as a defensible approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (Biscuits and cookies) used as the closest defensible category for French macaron; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for macaron specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for macaron; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard French macaron weighs approximately 15 g and is typically consumed as a single-bite confection or as part of a small assortment of 2–3 pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Macaron — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Macaron (cr_id F11080034). All core macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron values are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Macarons, French almond meringue cookies' (FDC ID 2003586) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for French macaron in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous confectionery items with comparable sugar (35–40 g/100g) and almond flour composition. Almond flour lowers the GI relative to wheat-flour cookies (typical GI 70–75) due to fat and fibre content; a GI of 65 is a conservative medium estimate consistent with similar almond-based confections in the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving = (65 × 8.6 g carbohydrate per 15 g serving) / 100 = 5.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (35.5 g/100g) and moderate sodium (234 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular macaron consumption; the high added-sugar load (35.5 g/100g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and raises blood pressure risk independently of the moderate sodium content (234 mg/100g), per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines on added sugars and cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (35.5 g/100g) causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Macarons are categorically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the sugar content of 35.5 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling, and even a single 15 g macaron delivers approximately 5.3 g of sugar, producing a significant postprandial glucose spike that undermines glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) and high added sugar (35.5 g/100g) contributing to dyslipidaemia and endothelial stress
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should strictly limit macaron intake; the estimated saturated fat content of approximately 8.5 g/100g from butter and cream exceeds the AHA recommended daily saturated fat ceiling for CVD patients, and the high added-sugar load further promotes triglyceride elevation and endothelial inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar (35.5 g/100g) and saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid macarons; the combination of 35.5 g/100g refined sugar and approximately 8.5 g/100g saturated fat promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, exacerbating joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A macaron (French macaron) is a classic French confection consisting of two light, crisp almond meringue shells sandwiched together with a creamy filling such as ganache, buttercream, or jam. The shells are made from almond flour, icing sugar, and egg whites whipped to a meringue, then piped into rounds and baked until they develop a characteristic smooth top and ruffled 'foot'. The filling is typically rich in butter or cream. Macarons are known for their delicate crunch giving way to a chewy interior, and they are available in a wide range of flavours and colours. Per 100 g, macarons provide approximately 488 kcal, 25.6 g fat (of which approximately 8.5 g is saturated), 57.3 g carbohydrate, 35.5 g sugar, 5.1 g protein, 8.6 g dietary fibre (from almond flour), and 234 mg sodium. The high sugar content (35.5 g/100g) makes macarons unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The almond flour base contributes meaningful dietary fibre and some protein, but the dominant added-sugar and saturated-fat profile overrides these benefits in a clinical context. Macarons are a popular premium snack and gift item in Singapore and globally, often sold in boutique patisseries and hotel lobbies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a macaron?
What is the difference between a macaron and a macaroon?
How much sugar is in a macaron?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry. GI estimated from analogous confectionery data in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. All primary sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: iron_mg is naturally zero as this is a confectionery item with no iron-rich ingredients, confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item (cr_id F11080034). saturated_fat_g (8.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for French almond meringue cookies (FDC ID 2003586) as the HPB record does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for macaron are not reported in the HPB Foodo database, and the USDA FoodData Central entry for this item does not provide a complete micronutrient breakdown as of April 2026. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as macarons are not a wholegrain food; almond flour is not a wholegrain ingredient. The 'high_fibre' tag is retained as dietary fibre is 8.6 g/100g, which meets the high-fibre threshold. Macaron is a Western (French) dish and does not require Singaporean multilingual coverage (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual rule; however, Chinese simplified and traditional names are provided as value-add given the food's popularity in Singapore. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for French macaron should be sought from the University of Sydney GI Database in future updates.
